article.title: Anthropic Rents All of SpaceX's Colossus 1 in Memphis, Adding 300 MW and 220,000 Nvidia GPUs to Cope With 80-Fold Quarterly Growth
article.summary: On May 6, Anthropic struck a deal to take over all compute capacity at SpaceX's Colossus 1 data center, enabling doubled Claude Code rate limits and an Opus API uplift to absorb a Q1 surge CEO Dario Amodei called 'just crazy.'
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Anthropic announced on May 6 that it has signed a deal with SpaceX to use the entire compute capacity of the Colossus 1 data center in Memphis, Tennessee, gaining more than 300 megawatts of new capacity and over 220,000 Nvidia GPUs within the month, [according to Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex). The arrangement, announced alongside the company's developer day, hands a former rival's flagship supercomputer to Anthropic at a moment when CEO Dario Amodei says quarterly growth is outrunning the company's compute plans.

## What We Know

The Colossus 1 facility houses more than 220,000 Nvidia GPUs, including H100 and H200 chips alongside the next-generation GB200 accelerators, [as reported by Capacity](https://capacityglobal.com/news/anthropic-secures-full-capacity-of-spacex-data-centre/). The new capacity will come online within the month and is expected to directly improve service for Claude Pro and Claude Max subscribers, [per Anthropic's announcement](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex).

Alongside the infrastructure deal, Anthropic announced three changes to Claude usage limits, [according to Engadget](https://www.engadget.com/2166315/anthropic-is-doubling-claude-code-rate-limits-after-deal-with-spacex/): Claude Code's five-hour rate limits are doubled for Pro, Max, Team, and seat-based Enterprise plans; the peak-hours limit reduction on Claude Code has been removed for Pro and Max users; and API rate limits for Claude Opus models have been raised "considerably."

Tom Brown, Anthropic's co-founder, framed the urgency directly. "In the next few days, we'll be ramping up Claude inference on Colossus. Grateful to be partnering with SpaceX here," he said, [as quoted by Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/sectors/technology/articles/elon-musks-spacex-help-power-202319358.html).

The deal is striking because Anthropic and xAI — the AI lab Elon Musk merged into SpaceX — are direct competitors. Musk had previously written on X that Anthropic "hates Western Civilization." Explaining his reversal, he said: "Everyone I met was highly competent and cared a great deal about doing the right thing. No one set off my evil detector," [Yahoo Finance reported](https://finance.yahoo.com/sectors/technology/articles/elon-musks-spacex-help-power-202319358.html). Musk also said SpaceX reserves "the right to reclaim the compute if their AI engages in actions that harm humanity," [per Engadget](https://www.engadget.com/2166315/anthropic-is-doubling-claude-code-rate-limits-after-deal-with-spacex/).

The driver, on Anthropic's side, is demand. Anthropic's annualized revenue has climbed to $30 billion, a three-fold increase year over year, and CEO Dario Amodei said the company experienced 80-fold growth in revenue and usage during the first quarter on an annualized basis, [Fortune reported](https://fortune.com/2026/05/08/anthropic-80fold-growth-quarter-renting-elon-musk-data-center/). Amodei called the surge "just crazy" and "too hard to handle," adding: "That is the reason we have had difficulties with compute," [according to Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/05/08/anthropic-80fold-growth-quarter-renting-elon-musk-data-center/).

Colossus 1 is located in Memphis, Tennessee, [as reported by Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/economy/2026/5/6/spacex-backs-anthropic-with-data-centre-deal-amidst-musks-openai-lawsuit). The facility was Musk's first xAI training cluster; xAI has since shifted training workloads to a separate Colossus 2 site in the same city, freeing the original campus for the Anthropic agreement.

As part of the agreement, Anthropic also expressed interest in partnering with SpaceX to develop multiple gigawatts of orbital AI compute capacity, [per Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex). The Machine Herald [previously reported](/article/2026-02/18-spacex-asks-the-fcc-to-approve-one-million-orbital-data-center-satellites-claiming-ai-compute-will-be-cheapest-in-space) on SpaceX's January FCC filing seeking permission to deploy up to one million solar-powered orbital data center satellites.

The Colossus 1 deal is one in a string of large infrastructure agreements Anthropic has signed in recent months. The company's announcement also references partnerships with Amazon for up to 5 GW with nearly 1 GW by end of 2026, with Google for 5 GW beginning in 2027, with Microsoft and Nvidia for $30 billion in Azure capacity, and a $50 billion Fluidstack commitment to American AI infrastructure, [per Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/news/higher-limits-spacex).

## What We Don't Know

Financial terms of the SpaceX agreement were not disclosed. Neither Anthropic nor SpaceX has published a duration, an annual fee, or a structure for revenue sharing on any orbital follow-on work. The companies have also not detailed how Anthropic's safety commitments will be operationalized against Musk's stated reclaim clause, or what process would trigger it.

The practical timing of the GPU ramp also remains imprecise: Anthropic and SpaceX have said capacity comes online "within the month," while Brown framed inference rollout as taking "the next few days," [per Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/sectors/technology/articles/elon-musks-spacex-help-power-202319358.html). The two timelines are not necessarily contradictory — partial inference traffic could begin while full capacity is still being commissioned — but neither company has offered a phased breakdown.

Finally, Colossus 1 has been the subject of community concerns about gas-turbine power generation and air quality in southwest Memphis. Neither Anthropic nor SpaceX addressed those concerns in the announcement materials reviewed for this article.
